title: What is Feature Engineering?
concept: Feature engineering is the process of turning raw data into the input variables — 'features' — that a model can actually learn from.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the clearest diagnostic sign that a model is overfitting?

options.text: A high training score paired with a much lower validation or test score
Low scores on both training and test data
The model trains very quickly

exercises.explain: Right! Now let's zoom out from training itself and ask a bigger question: what are we even feeding the model in the first place?

exercises.prompt: True or false: Gathering more varied training data is one general fix for an overfitting model.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! And that raises a question we haven't asked yet: what actually goes INTO a model besides raw data? Today, we find out.

cards.title: A fridge full of ingredients
cards.body: Open a fridge and you'll find raw ingredients: a whole onion, an uncracked egg, a block of cheese. Nobody eats the fridge's contents directly — someone has to chop, mix, and cook them into an actual meal first.

cards.title: What is feature engineering?
cards.body: Feature engineering is the process of turning raw data into features — the specific input variables a model uses to learn patterns and make predictions.

cards.title: Raw ingredients vs. a prepared meal
cards.body: Raw data is the fridge full of ingredients. A model can't 'eat' that directly — it needs a prepared meal: ingredients chopped, combined, and portioned into something it can actually digest. That preparation step is feature engineering.

cards.title: From watch logs to features
cards.body: A streaming app has raw logs of every second a user watched. On their own, those logs are just a giant pile of timestamps. Feature engineering turns them into usable inputs like average watch time, favorite genre, and days active this week.

cards.title: It's not just "collect more data"
cards.body: It's tempting to think the fix for a weak model is always more rows of raw data. But a bigger pile of unprepared ingredients is still not a meal — often what's missing isn't more raw data, it's better-prepared features from the data you already have.

cards.title: Raw Data vs. Features
cards.body: What a model receives before vs. after feature engineering:

left.text:
Unprepared logs
Timestamps, IDs, text
Not directly usable

right.text:
Prepared input variables
e.g. avg_watch_time
Ready for a model to learn from

options.text: Turning raw data into the input variables a model can learn from
Choosing which algorithm to train
Deleting a model once it's no longer needed
Deploying a trained model to a live app

exercises.explain: Exactly! Feature engineering is the preparation step that transforms raw data into usable inputs, or 'features.'

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model can typically learn just as well from raw, unprepared data as it can from well-engineered features.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Raw data is usually not directly usable — like ingredients in a fridge, it needs to be prepared into features first.

exercises.sentence: Feature engineering turns raw data into the input ___ a model actually learns from.
exercises.explain: Right! 'Features' is the name for the prepared input variables a model uses.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of a well-engineered feature, rather than raw data?

options.text: average_watch_time_minutes, calculated from a user's raw viewing logs
A single giant text file of every second-by-second timestamp a user watched
A random unique ID number assigned to each user

exercises.explain: Correct! average_watch_time_minutes is a prepared, meaningful input — the raw log dump and a random ID aren't directly useful signals.

exercises.prompt: A team has millions of rows of raw, unprepared sensor data but their model still performs poorly. What does today's lesson suggest they should consider first?

options.text: Whether they've actually turned that raw data into useful, prepared features yet
Buying an even bigger computer to store more raw rows
Deleting the raw data entirely
Nothing — more raw rows always fixes weak performance

exercises.explain: Right! Volume of raw data isn't the same as quality of features — the fridge full of ingredients still needs to be turned into a meal.

exercises.prompt: A startup dumps a raw spreadsheet of customer sign-up timestamps and click logs straight into a model with no preparation, and it performs terribly. A teammate says 'we just need a fancier algorithm.' What does today's lesson suggest instead?

options.text: The raw data likely needs to be engineered into meaningful features first — like a fridge of ingredients needs to become a meal
The teammate is right — a fancier algorithm is always the correct first move
Nothing can be done; raw timestamps and click logs are permanently unusable
They should collect even more raw, unprepared rows before trying anything else

exercises.explain: Exactly! Before reaching for a fancier algorithm, ask whether the raw data has actually been turned into usable features yet.

exercises.prompt: Which pair correctly matches the fridge/meal analogy to feature engineering?

options.text: Fridge full of ingredients = raw data; prepared meal = engineered features
Fridge full of ingredients = the trained model; prepared meal = raw data
Prepared meal = the algorithm choice; fridge = the deployed app
Both the fridge and the meal represent the same thing: the algorithm

exercises.explain: Correct! Raw data is the unprepared ingredients; features are what you get once that data has been prepared for a model to consume. Next: what happens when the ingredients themselves are bad?
